lem:finite-dimensional-supporting-hyperp.proof_tex: Source/proof. This is the finite-dimensional strict separation/supporting-hyperplane theorem for convex sets; see Rockafellar, Convex Analysis, Princeton University Press, 1970, Theorem 11.3 and Corollary 11.6.1. Applied to a compact convex set and a disjoint open convex ray, it gives the separating affine functional used in the bounded moment-slice duality lemma.

snapshot.stmt: If C is a compact convex subset of R^n and an open convex set O is disjoint from C, then there is a nonzero affine functional that weakly separates C from O; in particular, a finite-dimensional compact convex moment set has a supporting hyperplane at any exposed lower boundary point.

lem:bounded-moment-slice-duality.proof_tex:
Proof. The sufficiency direction is immediate. If Q_{a,b,c}>=0 on D_pbar, then for any centered law mu with int d dmu=0 and int d^2 dmu=s,
 int f dmu = a + b int d dmu + c int d^2 dmu + int Q dmu >= a + c s.
If Q vanishes on the support of mu_star, equality holds for mu_star, so mu_star minimizes the slice objective.
For necessity, pass to centered laws on the compact interval D_pbar. Let C be the compact convex subset of R^4 consisting of all vectors (int 1 dmu, int d dmu, int d^2 dmu, int f dmu) as mu ranges over probability laws on D_pbar. If mu_star is a slice minimizer at s, then the point z_star=(1,0,s,F(s)) lies on the lower boundary of C above the affine coordinates (1,0,s). Separating C from the open ray {(1,0,s,y): y<F(s)} gives a supporting hyperplane whose coefficient on the fourth coordinate is positive; normalizing that coefficient to one yields constants a,b,c with f(d)-a-bd-cd^2>=0 for every d in D_pbar. Integrating against mu_star gives zero integral of this nonnegative continuous function. A nonnegative continuous function with zero integral under mu_star must vanish on the topological support of mu_star. Translating the support back by T_pbar gives the stated support condition.

snapshot.stmt: Fix s for which N_s(pbar,s) is nonempty and set f(d)=V_1d^2+V_3d^3+V_4d^4. A law nu_star in N_s(pbar,s) minimizes int f(d) d(T_pbar)_#nu(d) over N_s(pbar,s) if and only if there exist a,b,c in R such that Q_{a,b,c}(d)=f(d)-a-bd-cd^2 is nonnegative on D_pbar and vanishes on T_pbar(supp(nu_star)).

lem:extreme-moment-slice-support.proof_tex: Source/proof. This is the standard extreme-point theorem for moment sets. See Winkler, Extreme points of moment sets, Mathematics of Operations Research 13(4), 1988, Theorem 2.1, which characterizes extreme points of sets of probability measures with finitely many generalized moment constraints; with the three functions 1,d,d^2, an extreme probability measure has at most three atoms. The existence of an extreme minimizer of a continuous affine functional on a nonempty compact convex moment set is the Bauer maximum principle applied to the negative of the functional, or equivalently the Krein-Milman theorem followed by affinity of the objective. These cited results supply exactly the compact interval, three-moment version used here.

snapshot.stmt: In the compact interval D_pbar, every nonempty moment slice of probability laws satisfying int 1 dmu=1, int d dmu=0, and int d^2 dmu=s has an extreme point minimizer for each continuous linear objective, and every such extreme point is supported on at most three points.

lem:centered-pushforward-program.proof_tex: Let mu=(T_pbar)_#nu. Since nu is supported on [0,1], T_pbar sends [0,1] into D_pbar=[-pbar,1-pbar], so mu is supported on D_pbar. By the change-of-variables formula, int d dmu(d)=int (u-pbar)dnu(u)=int u dnu(u)-pbar=0 because nu belongs to N(pbar). The same change of variables gives int d^r dmu(d)=int (u-pbar)^r dnu(u)=m_r(nu) for r=2,3,4. Conversely, if mu is supported on D_pbar and has mean zero, then the pushforward nu under d -> pbar+d is supported on [0,1] and int u dnu(u)=int (pbar+d)dmu(d)=pbar, so nu is in N(pbar). Finally, for U in [0,1] with law nu and E U=pbar, m_2(nu)=E[(U-pbar)^2]=E[U^2]-pbar^2<=E[U]-pbar^2=pbar(1-pbar), while nonnegativity is immediate.

snapshot.stmt: For 0<pbar<1, the map T_pbar(u)=u-pbar sends each nu in N(pbar) to a probability law mu on D_pbar with int d dmu(d)=0, and m_r(nu)=int d^r dmu(d) for r=2,3,4. Conversely, each probability law mu on D_pbar with int d dmu(d)=0 pushes forward under d -> pbar+d to a law in N(pbar). For every nu in N(pbar), 0<=m_2(nu)<=pbar(1-pbar).

defs.def:admissible-law-class: For pbar in [0,1], N(pbar) = {nu in P([0,1]) : integral_[0,1] u d nu(u) = pbar}.
defs.def:centered-moments: For r in {2,3,4}, m_r(nu) = integral_[0,1] (u - pbar)^r d nu(u).

lem:profile-value-reduction.proof_tex: Fix nu in N_s(pbar,s) and set mu=(T_pbar)_#nu. By lem:centered-pushforward-program and def:centered-moments, m_2(nu)=s and m_r(nu)=int d^r dmu(d) for r=2,3,4. Substituting into def:variance-functional yields V(nu)=V_0+int [V_1d^2+V_3d^3+V_4d^4]dmu(d)-V_4 s^2. The term V_0-V_4s^2 is constant on the slice. Taking the infimum over nu in N_s(pbar,s) and using def:profile-value gives inf_{nu in N_s(pbar,s)}V(nu)=V_0+F(s)-V_4s^2. By def:outer-certificate, the right side is H(s).

snapshot.stmt: Let f(d)=V_1 d^2+V_3 d^3+V_4 d^4 and H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4 s^2. For every nonempty slice N_s(pbar,s), H(s)=inf{V(nu):nu in N_s(pbar,s)}. Moreover, for every nu in N_s(pbar,s), V(nu)=V_0+int f(d)d(T_pbar)_#nu(d)-V_4 s^2.

defs.def:centered-moments: For r in {2,3,4}, m_r(nu) = integral_[0,1] (u - pbar)^r d nu(u).
defs.def:profile-value: F(s) = inf_{nu in N_s(pbar,s)} integral_[0,1] [V_1 (u - pbar)^2 + V_3 (u - pbar)^3 + V_4 (u - pbar)^4] d nu(u).
defs.def:outer-certificate: For the necessary-and-sufficient global certificate, the outer certificate is the profiled value H(s) = V_0 + F(s) - V_4 s^2. The affine expression G^{lin}_{a,c}(s)=V_0+a+c s-V_4s^2 is only a sufficient lower-certificate obtained from one slice dual line; it is not necessary for global optimality.

lem:compact-moment-program-attains.proof_tex: The space of probability laws on the compact interval [0,1] is weakly compact by the standard compactness theorem for probability measures on compact metric spaces. The maps nu -> int u dnu(u) and nu -> int (u-pbar)^r dnu(u), r=2,3,4, are continuous because their integrands are continuous and bounded. Hence N(pbar), cut out by the closed mean constraint int u dnu=pbar, is compact. Likewise N_s(pbar,s), adding the closed second-moment constraint, is compact whenever nonempty. The formula in def:variance-functional is a continuous function of the moments m_2,m_3,m_4, so Weierstrass gives a minimizer of V on N(pbar). The profile objective in def:profile-value is also the integral of a continuous bounded polynomial in u-pbar, hence continuous on the compact slice N_s(pbar,s), so its infimum is attained.

snapshot.stmt: For 0<pbar<1, the objective V attains a minimum over N(pbar). For every s in [0,pbar(1-pbar)] with N_s(pbar,s) nonempty, the profile value F(s) is attained on N_s(pbar,s).

defs.def:centered-moments: For r in {2,3,4}, m_r(nu) = integral_[0,1] (u - pbar)^r d nu(u).

lem:pearson-centered-moment-bound.proof_tex: Because s>0, the measure rho defined by drho(d)=d^2 dmu(d)/s is a probability law. Its support is contained in D_pbar, so its mean t=int d drho(d)=m_3/s lies in the convex hull of D_pbar, which is D_pbar itself. The Cauchy-Schwarz inequality applied to the square-integrable random variables d^2 and 1 under mu gives (int d^2 dmu)^2 <= int d^4 dmu. A sharper form applied to d^2-s and d gives (int (d^2-s)d dmu)^2 <= int (d^2-s)^2 dmu * int d^2 dmu. Since int d dmu=0, the left side is m_3^2 and the first factor on the right is m_4-s^2. Thus m_3^2 <= (m_4-s^2)s, which is the claimed inequality.

snapshot.stmt: Let mu be a probability law on D_pbar with int d dmu(d)=0, let s=int d^2 dmu(d)>0, m_3=int d^3 dmu(d), and m_4=int d^4 dmu(d). Then t=m_3/s lies in D_pbar and m_4-s^2>=m_3^2/s.

node.statement: Let mu be a probability law on D_pbar with int d dmu(d)=0, let s=int d^2 dmu(d)>0, m_3=int d^3 dmu(d), and m_4=int d^4 dmu(d). Then t=m_3/s lies in D_pbar and m_4-s^2>=m_3^2/s.

lem:second-moment-slices-nonempty.proof_tex:
Let D_pbar=[-pbar,1-pbar]. The centered two-endpoint law

mu_max = (1-pbar) delta_{-pbar} + pbar delta_{1-pbar}

has mean zero because (1-pbar)(-pbar)+pbar(1-pbar)=0, and second moment pbar(1-pbar). For s in [0,pbar(1-pbar)], put lambda=s/[pbar(1-pbar)] and define

mu_s = (1-lambda) delta_0 + lambda mu_max.

Then mu_s is supported on D_pbar, has mean zero, and has second moment s. Push mu_s forward by d -> pbar+d to obtain nu_s on [0,1]. By lem:centered-pushforward-program, nu_s lies in N(pbar), and by construction m_2(nu_s)=s. Hence nu_s is in N_s(pbar,s).

snapshot.stmt: For 0<pbar<1 and every s in [0,pbar(1-pbar)], the slice N_s(pbar,s) is nonempty.

lem:two-point-centered-objective.proof_tex:
The centered mean is

[b/(a+b)]a + [a/(a+b)](-b)=0,

so the pushforward law nu has mean pbar and hence belongs to N(pbar). Direct calculation gives

m_2 = [b/(a+b)]a^2 + [a/(a+b)]b^2 = ab,

m_3 = [b/(a+b)]a^3 - [a/(a+b)]b^3 = ab(a-b),

and

m_4 = [b/(a+b)]a^4 + [a/(a+b)]b^4 = ab(a^2-ab+b^2).

Therefore

m_4-m_2^2 = ab(a^2-ab+b^2)-a^2b^2 = ab(a-b)^2.

Substituting these three identities into def:variance-functional yields

V(nu)-V_0 = V_1 ab + V_3 ab(a-b) + V_4 ab(a-b)^2 = ab[V_1+V_3(a-b)+V_4(a-b)^2].

snapshot.stmt: Let 0<a<=1-pbar and 0<b<=pbar. Let mu place mass b/(a+b) at a and mass a/(a+b) at -b, and let nu be its pushforward under d -> pbar+d. Then nu in N(pbar), m_2(nu)=ab, m_3(nu)=ab(a-b), m_4(nu)-m_2(nu)^2=ab(a-b)^2, and V(nu)-V_0=ab[V_1+V_3(a-b)+V_4(a-b)^2].

lem:cai-randomized-saturation-moment-exp.proof_tex: Source proof. Cai, Pouget-Abadie, and Airoldi, "Optimizing Randomized and Deterministic Saturation Designs under Interference," arXiv:2203.09682, Theorem 9, Eq. (4), states the displayed expansion of the design variance of the difference-in-means estimator in terms of the centered second, third, and fourth moments of the treatment-proportion vector, with an o(N^-1) remainder in the regime M -> infinity and inf_j N_j -> infinity. Their Section 2.4.2 and Corollary 12 give the simplified coefficient formulas under the block-fixed specialization, and the discussion immediately following Corollary 12 states that in the extreme case of isolated clusters the V_2 coefficient is zero. This cited theorem and corollary are the published source for the expansion used here.

snapshot.stmt: Cai-Pouget-Abadie-Airoldi randomized-saturation variance expansion. For an equal-cluster randomized-saturation design under the linear interference model and the regularity regime M -> infinity and inf_j N_j -> infinity used in Cai, Pouget-Abadie, and Airoldi (2022), if pi is the cluster treatment-proportion vector with mean pbar, mu_2(pi) is its centered second moment, and mu_3(pi), mu_4(pi) are its centered third and fourth moments, then Var_Z[hat_tau] = V_0 + V_1 mu_2(pi) + V_2 mu_2(pi)^2 + V_3 mu_3(pi) + V_4 (mu_4(pi) - mu_2(pi)^2) + o(N^-1). Under the block-fixed isolated-cluster specialization, the coefficient V_2 is zero.

lem:empirical-centered-moment-continuity.proof_tex: Proof. For each r in {2,3,4}, the function phi_r(u) = (u-pbar)^r is continuous and bounded on the compact interval [0,1]. Weak convergence of L_M(pi^(M)) to nu therefore implies integral phi_r dL_M(pi^(M)) -> integral phi_r dnu. By the definition of the empirical law, integral phi_r dL_M(pi^(M)) = M^(-1) sum_{j=1}^M (pi_j^(M)-pbar)^r. If nu in N(pbar), def:centered-moments identifies the limiting integral with m_r(nu).

snapshot.stmt: Let pi^(M) be saturation vectors in [0,1]^M whose empirical laws L_M(pi^(M)) converge weakly to nu in P([0,1]), and let pbar be fixed. Then for r in {2,3,4}, M^(-1) sum_{j=1}^M (pi_j^(M)-pbar)^r -> integral_[0,1] (u-pbar)^r d nu(u). In particular, if nu in N(pbar), the limit is m_r(nu).

defs.def:admissible-law-class: For pbar in [0,1], N(pbar) = {nu in P([0,1]) : integral_[0,1] u d nu(u) = pbar}.

lem:centered-second-moment-extrema.proof_tex: Proof. Let U denote the identity random variable on [0,1] under the law nu. By def:admissible-law-class, E_nu[U]=pbar, and by def:centered-moments, m_2(nu)=E_nu[(U-pbar)^2]. The lower bound m_2(nu)>=0 follows from nonnegativity of the square. Equality holds if and only if U=pbar nu-almost surely, which is exactly nu=delta_pbar; conversely delta_pbar is admissible and has m_2(delta_pbar)=0. For the upper bound, expand m_2(nu)=E_nu[U^2]-2pbar E_nu[U]+pbar^2=E_nu[U^2]-pbar^2. Since 0<=U<=1, U^2<=U pointwise, so E_nu[U^2]<=E_nu[U]=pbar. Hence m_2(nu)<=pbar-pbar^2=pbar(1-pbar). For nu_B=(1-pbar)delta_0+pbar delta_1, E_{nu_B}[U]=pbar and E_{nu_B}[U^2]=pbar, so m_2(nu_B)=pbar(1-pbar), proving attainment. If 0<pbar<1 and equality holds in the upper bound, then equality must hold in U^2<=U nu-almost surely, so U belongs to {0,1} nu-almost surely. The mean constraint then forces nu({1})=pbar and nu({0})=1-pbar, hence nu=nu_B.

lem:semialgebraic-optimization-algorithm.proof_tex: Proof. This is the standard algorithmic real algebraic geometry substrate: Tarski's decision method for real closed fields (Tarski, 1951), Seidenberg's quantifier-elimination theorem for real closed fields (Seidenberg, 1954), Collins's cylindrical algebraic decomposition with sample points and Thom encodings (Collins, 1975), and the classical Sturm theorem for counting and ordering real roots of univariate polynomials. These results imply decidability of finite systems of polynomial equalities and inequalities, construction of algebraic sample points in each cell, exact comparison of algebraic objective values, and verification of polynomial nonnegativity on compact intervals.

snapshot.stmt: Finite polynomial optimization over compact semialgebraic subsets of a real closed field generated by exact input parameters admits a terminating exact procedure that returns algebraic sample minimizers, exact ordered objective values, and certificates of univariate polynomial nonnegativity on intervals via Thom encodings, cylindrical algebraic decomposition, quantifier elimination, real-root isolation, and Sturm sign tests.

lem:two-point-endpoint-reduction.proof_tex: Proof. Any nondegenerate two-point mean-zero centered law has support {-b,a} with 0<a<=q and 0<b<=p and unique weights b/(a+b) at a and a/(a+b) at -b. By lem:two-point-centered-objective, V-V_0=ab q_theta(a-b). Put t=a-b. For fixed t, feasibility is b in [max(0,-t), min(p,q-t)] and a=b+t. The product ab=b(b+t) is increasing over this feasible interval, so its maximum is obtained at b=p when t<=A and at a=q when t>=A. If q_theta(t)<0, the smallest value for that t uses this maximum product; if q_theta(t)>=0, the degenerate singleton value 0 is no worse. Therefore no two-point law improves on the union of the singleton and the two endpoint branches, and every point on those branches is itself a feasible two-point or limiting singleton law.

snapshot.stmt: For 0<pbar<1, with p=pbar, q=1-p, A=q-p, and q_theta(t)=V_1+V_3t+V_4t^2, the minimum of V-V_0 over mean-zero laws supported on at most two centered points in [-p,q] equals the minimum over the singleton value 0 and the two endpoint branches g_L(t)=p(p+t)q_theta(t) for t in [-p,A] and g_R(t)=q(q-t)q_theta(t) for t in [A,q].

lem:compact-convex-moment-slice.proof_tex: Proof. Because D_pbar is compact, P(D_pbar) is weakly compact by Prokhorov compactness on compact metric spaces, equivalently by the Riesz representation theorem and Banach-Alaoglu. The maps mu |-> integral d dmu and mu |-> integral d^2 dmu are continuous for weak convergence because d and d^2 are bounded continuous functions on D_pbar. Hence the slice is a closed subset of the compact set P(D_pbar), so it is compact. It is convex because the two moment constraints are affine in mu. A continuous affine functional is continuous on this compact set, so the Weierstrass theorem gives an attained minimum.

snapshot.stmt: For a compact interval D_pbar and any s for which the centered slice {mu in P(D_pbar): integral d dmu=0, integral d^2 dmu=s} is nonempty, that slice is compact and convex in the weak topology, and every continuous affine functional on it attains its minimum.

lem:fixed-second-moment-envelope.proof_tex: Proof. Let X have law mu on D=[-p,q]. First bound m3. Since (q-X)(X+s/q)^2>=0 on D, expansion and E X=0 give 0<=E[(q-X)(X+s/q)^2]=q s-m3-s^2/q, hence t=m3/s<=q-s/q. Since (X+p)(X-s/p)^2>=0 on D, 0<=E[(X+p)(X-s/p)^2]=m3+p s-s^2/p, hence t>=s/p-p. Thus t lies in I_2(s). Because m3=s t=A s+(s-C)r, the affine relation t=A+(s-C)r/s maps r=-s/q to q-s/q and r=s/p to s/p-p; since s-C<0, this is equivalent to r in I_3(s). For the lower envelope, expand (X^2-tX-s)^2. Using E X=0 and m3=s t gives E[(X^2-tX-s)^2]=m4-s^2-s t^2, so m4>=s^2+s t^2. Equality holds iff X^2-tX-s=0 mu-a.s.; the endpoint inequalities above are exactly the condition that the two roots lie in [-p,q]. For the upper envelope, (X+p)(q-X)(X-r)^2 is nonnegative on [-p,q]. Since (X+p)(q-X)=C+A X-X^2 and m3=A s+(s-C)r, expansion gives E[(X+p)(q-X)(X-r)^2]=(1-3C)s+(s-C)(r^2+A r)-m4. Hence m4 is bounded above by the displayed expression. Equality holds iff X is supported on {-p,r,q}, with deletion of zero-weight or coincident endpoint cases. Solving w_-+w_r+w_+=1, -p w_-+r w_r+q w_+=0, and p^2w_-+r^2w_r+q^2w_+=s gives w_-=(s+rq)/(p+r), w_r=(C-s)/((p+r)(q-r)), and w_+=(s-pr)/(q-r). The inequalities r in [-s/q,s/p] make these weights nonnegative, with endpoint limiting interpretations.

lem:finite-atomic-grid-split-rounding.proof_tex: Choose integers n_1,...,n_K with sum_ell n_ell = M and |n_ell/M - w_ell| <= 1/M for every ell, for example by the usual largest-remainder rounding of the masses. Let nu_M^0 = sum_ell (n_ell/M) delta_{u_ell}. Since nu and nu_M^0 are supported on the same K points, couple the common mass at each point to itself and move only the excess mass. The total excess mass is at most sum_ell |n_ell/M - w_ell| <= K/M, and all moves occur inside [0,1], so the transport cost from nu to nu_M^0 is at most K/M. Now form an M-vector x by listing n_ell copies of u_ell for each ell. Its empirical law is nu_M^0. Replace each x_j by y_j = floor(m x_j)/m, with the convention y_j=1 when x_j=1. Then y_j belongs to {0,1/m,...,1}, |y_j-x_j| <= 1/m, and the paired empirical laws have average displacement at most 1/m. Let S = N pbar, an integer by hypothesis, and let T = sum_{j=1}^M m y_j. If S > T, increase grid coordinates one unit at a time, never above m; if S < T, decrease grid coordinates one unit at a time, never below 0. This is feasible because 0 <= S <= M m, so the total upward capacity is M m - T >= S - T when S >= T, and the total downward capacity is T >= T - S when S <= T. Let z_j be the resulting grid coordinates divided by m, and set pi_rd=(z_1,...,z_M). Then pi_rd is on the 1/m grid and M^{-1} sum_j pi_rd,j = S/(M m)=pbar, so pi_rd lies in Pi_{M,m}(pbar). This is exactly the mass-round, grid-split, and final-balancing procedure described in def:grid-split-rounding. The cost of the final balancing coupling from the empirical law of y to the empirical law of z is M^{-1} sum_j |z_j-y_j| = |S-T|/(M m). Since nu has mean pbar and |M^{-1} sum_j x_j - pbar| <= K/M while |M^{-1} sum_j y_j - M^{-1} sum_j x_j| <= 1/m, we have |S-T|/(M m) = |pbar - M^{-1} sum_j y_j| <= K/M + 1/m. Concatenating the three couplings gives a coupling of nu and L_M(pi_rd) with cost at most K/M + 1/m + K/M + 1/m = 2K/M + 2/m. Since K <= 3, this is at most 6/M + 2/m.

snapshot.stmt: Let nu = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell} be in N(pbar), with K <= 3, M,m positive integers, N=Mm, and N pbar in Z. The grid-split rounding handle can be run to produce pi_rd in Pi_{M,m}(pbar) and a coupling gamma_rd of nu and L_M(pi_rd) such that integral |u-v| d gamma_rd(u,v) <= 2K/M + 2/m <= 6/M + 2/m.

lem:quartic-variance-coupling-lipschitz.proof_tex: For r=2,3,4 set g_r(u)=(u-pbar)^r. Because u and pbar lie in [0,1], |u-pbar| <= 1, and the derivative bound |g_r'(u)| = r |u-pbar|^{r-1} <= r gives |g_r(u)-g_r(v)| <= r |u-v| for all u,v in [0,1]. Integrating this inequality under gamma yields |m_r(eta)-m_r(nu)| <= r Delta for r=2,3,4. Also 0 <= m_2(eta),m_2(nu) <= 1, so |m_2(eta)^2 - m_2(nu)^2| <= |m_2(eta)-m_2(nu)| |m_2(eta)+m_2(nu)| <= 2 |m_2(eta)-m_2(nu)| <= 4 Delta. Using def:variance-functional and cancelling V_0, we get |V(eta)-V(nu)| <= |V_1| |m_2(eta)-m_2(nu)| + |V_3| |m_3(eta)-m_3(nu)| + |V_4| (|m_4(eta)-m_4(nu)| + |m_2(eta)^2-m_2(nu)^2|) <= (2 |V_1| + 3 |V_3| + 8 |V_4|) Delta.

lem:boundary-admissible-law-singleton.proof_tex: Let nu be a probability law on [0,1] with integral u dnu(u)=pbar. If pbar=0, then u>=0 on [0,1] and a nonnegative random variable with mean zero is zero almost surely; hence nu=delta_0. If pbar=1, then 1-u>=0 and integral (1-u)dnu(u)=1-integral u dnu(u)=0, so 1-u=0 almost surely and nu=delta_1. The reverse inclusions delta_0 in N(0) and delta_1 in N(1) are immediate from the same mean constraint in def:admissible-law-class.

node.proof_tex: Let nu be a probability law on [0,1] with integral u dnu(u)=pbar. If pbar=0, then u>=0 on [0,1] and a nonnegative random variable with mean zero is zero almost surely; hence nu=delta_0. If pbar=1, then 1-u>=0 and integral (1-u)dnu(u)=1-integral u dnu(u)=0, so 1-u=0 almost surely and nu=delta_1. The reverse inclusions delta_0 in N(0) and delta_1 in N(1) are immediate from the same mean constraint in def:admissible-law-class.

lem:variance-moment-reduction.proof_tex:
Proof. Fix nu in N(pbar) and consider any sequence of finite two-stage randomized-saturation designs whose cluster saturation vectors pi have empirical laws L_M(pi) converging weakly to nu. Under ass:equal-cluster-size the clusters have common size m, so the cluster-level treatment-proportion vector pi is the object entering the randomized-saturation variance expansion. Under ass:isolated-partial-interference and ass:anonymous-share-specialization, the design contribution of assignments outside a cluster and all within-cluster assignment features other than the saturation share are absent from the leading criterion. Under ass:homogeneous-linear-share-working-model and ass:cai-leading-expansion-domain, the block-fixed isolated specialization is in the scope of Cai, Pouget-Abadie, and Airoldi's randomized-saturation expansion recorded in lem:cai-randomized-saturation-moment-expansion, with the V_2 coefficient equal to zero. Thus the o(N^{-1})-free leading term for a finite saturation vector pi is

V_0 + V_1 mu_2(pi) + V_3 mu_3(pi) + V_4{mu_4(pi)-mu_2(pi)^2},

where mu_r(pi)=M^{-1} sum_{j=1}^M (pi_j-pbar)^r for r=2,3,4. Since L_M(pi) converges weakly to nu and each function u -> (u-pbar)^r is continuous and bounded on [0,1], lem:empirical-centered-moment-continuity gives mu_r(pi) -> m_r(nu) for r=2,3,4. Passing to the law-level relaxation therefore gives the limiting o(N^{-1})-free leading criterion

V_0 + V_1 m_2(nu) + V_3 m_3(nu) + V_4{m_4(nu)-m_2(nu)^2}.

By def:variance-functional this expression is exactly V(nu). Hence, within this homogeneous isolated-share specialization, first-stage saturation optimization for the standard Cai leading design-variance criterion is precisely minimization of the quartic moment functional V over N(pbar). The assertion is only about the leading criterion after dropping the o(N^{-1}) remainder; the exact non-leading finite-population variance is not identified with V and remains outside this criterion-level statement.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:equal-cluster-size, ass:isolated-partial-interference, ass:anonymous-share-specialization, ass:homogeneous-linear-share-working-model, and ass:cai-leading-expansion-domain, for every nu in N(pbar), V(nu) is the standard finite-population design-variance criterion for first-stage saturation optimization: along finite randomized-saturation sequences whose saturation empirical law converges to nu, the o(N^{-1})-free leading term in Cai, Pouget-Abadie, and Airoldi's randomized-saturation variance expansion equals V(nu), with the V_2 coefficient equal to zero in the isolated homogeneous specialization. Thus the design-variance optimization target in this regime is exactly the quartic moment functional V; the separate non-leading finite-population variance is outside this criterion-level statement.

assumptions.ass:equal-cluster-size: The finite population is partitioned into M clusters C_1, ..., C_M of common size m.
assumptions.ass:isolated-partial-interference: Potential outcomes for units in cluster C_j depend only on assignments within C_j and not on assignments in C_l for l != j.
assumptions.ass:anonymous-share-specialization: Within each cluster, the working-model contribution of a realized assignment to the variance of hat_tau_DM depends on the assignment only through the cluster treatment share u.
assumptions.ass:homogeneous-linear-share-working-mod: The specialized within-cluster working model is homogeneous and linear in the centered share d = u - pbar, so the Cai-style design variance of hat_tau_DM is determined by finitely many centered share moments and coefficients V_0, V_1, V_3, V_4 that do not vary across clusters.
assumptions.ass:cai-leading-expansion-domain: The finite-population sequence used to define the randomized-saturation continuous-relaxation criterion is in the Cai-Pouget-Abadie-Airoldi asymptotic variance-expansion domain: M -> infinity, m -> infinity, the linear-interference potential-outcome parameters are uniformly bounded, and the regularity hypotheses of Cai, Pouget-Abadie, and Airoldi (2022, Theorem 9) hold in the homogeneous/block-fixed isolated-cluster specialization.

thm:global-certificate.proof_tex: Proof. Put f(d)=V_1 d^2+V_3 d^3+V_4 d^4 and let mu_star=(T_pbar)_#nu_star. By ass:budget-interior and lem:centered-pushforward-program, mu_star is a probability law on D_pbar with integral d dmu_star=0, and s_star=m_2(nu_star)=integral d^2 dmu_star. Also 0<=s_star<=pbar(1-pbar). By lem:profile-value-reduction, on each nonempty slice N_s(pbar,s) the value H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4 s^2 is exactly inf{V(nu):nu in N_s(pbar,s)}. First assume nu_star minimizes V over N(pbar). Then no law in its own slice can have smaller integral f, because V differs from integral f on that slice by the constant V_0 - V_4 s_star^2. Therefore nu_star is a fixed-s_star slice minimizer. By lem:bounded-moment-slice-duality there are a_star,b_star,c_star such that Q_{a_star,b_star,c_star}(d)>=0 for all d in D_pbar and Q_{a_star,b_star,c_star}(d)=0 on T_pbar(supp(nu_star)). Moreover, since nu_star is a global minimizer, every law in every slice has V at least V(nu_star). Taking the infimum over each slice and using lem:second-moment-slices-nonempty gives H(s)>=V(nu_star) for every s in [0,pbar(1-pbar)]. At s=s_star, nu_star itself belongs to the slice and is slice-minimizing, so H(s_star)=V(nu_star). Conversely, suppose the displayed quartic residual and outer-profile conditions hold. Let nu be arbitrary in N(pbar), and put s=m_2(nu). By lem:profile-value-reduction, V(nu)>=H(s). The assumed outer inequality gives H(s)>=V(nu_star). Hence V(nu)>=V(nu_star) for every admissible nu, so nu_star belongs to argmin_{nu in N(pbar)} V(nu). This proves both necessity and sufficiency.

snapshot.stmt: For nu_star in N(pbar), let s_star = m_2(nu_star), and write H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4 s^2 for the profiled outer value from def:outer-certificate. Then nu_star belongs to argmin_{nu in N(pbar)} V(nu) if and only if there exist scalars a_star, b_star, c_star such that Q_{a_star,b_star,c_star}(d) >= 0 for all d in D_pbar, Q_{a_star,b_star,c_star}(d) = 0 for every d in T_pbar(supp(nu_star)), and H(s) >= V(nu_star) for all s in [0, pbar(1-pbar)] with equality at s = s_star.

defs.def:admissible-law-class: For pbar in [0,1], N(pbar) = {nu in P([0,1]) : integral_[0,1] u d nu(u) = pbar}.
defs.def:centered-moments: For r in {2,3,4}, m_r(nu) = integral_[0,1] (u - pbar)^r d nu(u).
defs.def:outer-certificate: For the necessary-and-sufficient global certificate, the outer certificate is the profiled value H(s) = V_0 + F(s) - V_4 s^2. The affine expression G^{lin}_{a,c}(s)=V_0+a+c s-V_4s^2 is only a sufficient lower-certificate obtained from one slice dual line; it is not necessary for global optimality.

prop:support-at-most-three.proof_tex: Proof. If pbar=0 or pbar=1, then any law on [0,1] with mean pbar is delta_pbar, so the unique admissible law has support size one. Assume now 0<pbar<1. By lem:compact-moment-program-attains, V attains its minimum over N(pbar); take one minimizer nu_0 and set s_0=m_2(nu_0). Let mu_0=(T_pbar)_#nu_0. By lem:centered-pushforward-program, mu_0 is supported on D_pbar, has mean zero, and has second moment s_0. On the slice N_s(pbar,s_0), lem:profile-value-reduction shows that minimizing V is exactly the same as minimizing the continuous affine functional mu |-> integral (V_1 d^2+V_3 d^3+V_4 d^4) dmu, because V_0-V_4 s_0^2 is constant on that slice. By lem:compact-convex-moment-slice, the corresponding centered moment slice is nonempty, compact, and convex, and the affine objective attains its minimum there. By lem:extreme-moment-slice-support, the minimizing face has an extreme-point minimizer mu_star on the same slice, and every such extreme point is supported on at most three points. Push mu_star forward by d |-> pbar+d and call the resulting law nu_star. By lem:centered-pushforward-program, nu_star belongs to N(pbar), has the same second moment s_0, and attains the same slice infimum as nu_0. Since nu_0 was globally minimizing and the slice value at s_0 equals the global value, nu_star is also globally minimizing. Its support has cardinality at most that of mu_star, hence at most three.

snapshot.stmt: For the quartic saturation objective V over the admissible law class N(pbar), there exists nu_star in argmin_{nu in N(pbar)} V(nu) such that card(supp(nu_star)) <= 3.

prop:positive-penalty-threshold.proof_tex: Proof. Let q(d)=V_1+V_3 d+V_4 d^2. The law delta_pbar has m_2=m_3=m_4=0, hence V(delta_pbar)=V_0. Assume first that q(d)>=0 for every d in D_pbar. Let nu in N(pbar), let mu=(T_pbar)_#nu, set s=integral d^2 dmu, m_3=integral d^3 dmu, and m_4=integral d^4 dmu. If V_4<=0, then V(nu)-V_0=integral d^2 q(d)dmu - V_4 s^2>=0. If V_4>0 and s=0, all centered moments vanish and again V(nu)=V_0. If V_4>0 and s>0, lem:pearson-centered-moment-bound gives t=m_3/s in D_pbar and m_4-s^2>=m_3^2/s. Hence V(nu)-V_0=V_1 s+V_3 m_3+V_4(m_4-s^2)>=s[V_1+V_3 t+V_4 t^2]=s q(t)>=0. Thus no admissible law improves on delta_pbar. Conversely, suppose q(d_0)<0 for some d_0 in D_pbar. Since q is continuous and 0<pbar<1, choose t in the interior (-pbar,1-pbar) with q(t)<0. Choose a,b>0 with a-b=t, a<=1-pbar, and b<=pbar. For example, take b small when t>=0 and a small when t<0. Let mu put mass b/(a+b) at a and mass a/(a+b) at -b, and let nu be the pushforward under d -> pbar+d. By lem:two-point-centered-objective, nu belongs to N(pbar) and V(nu)-V_0=ab q(t)<0. Hence V(nu)<V(delta_pbar), so delta_pbar is not globally optimal. The two implications prove the iff threshold.

snapshot.stmt: For all real V_1, V_3, V_4 and every 0 < pbar < 1, delta_pbar belongs to argmin_{nu in N(pbar)} V(nu) if and only if min_{d in D_pbar} (V_1 + V_3 d + V_4 d^2) >= 0.

defs.def:admissible-law-class: For pbar in [0,1], N(pbar) = {nu in P([0,1]) : integral_[0,1] u d nu(u) = pbar}.

prop:skewed-witness.proof_tex: Proof. At pbar=1/3 the centered support points of nu_w=(2/3)delta_0+(1/3)delta_1 are -1/3 with mass 2/3 and 2/3 with mass 1/3. Therefore m_2(nu_w)=(2/3)(1/9)+(1/3)(4/9)=2/9, m_3(nu_w)=(2/3)(-1/27)+(1/3)(8/27)=2/27, and m_4(nu_w)=(2/3)(1/81)+(1/3)(16/81)=2/27. Thus m_4(nu_w)-m_2(nu_w)^2=2/27-4/81=2/81. Since delta_(1/3) has all centered moments equal to zero, for V_1=1,V_3=-10,V_4=1 we get V(nu_w)-V(delta_(1/3))=2/9-10(2/27)+2/81=(18-60+2)/81=-40/81<0.

snapshot.stmt: At pbar = 1/3, V_1 = 1, V_3 = -10, and V_4 = 1, the law nu_w = (2/3) delta_0 + (1/3) delta_1 satisfies m_2(nu_w) = 2/9, m_3(nu_w) = 2/27, m_4(nu_w) - m_2(nu_w)^2 = 2/81, and V(nu_w) - V(delta_(1/3)) = -40/81 < 0.

defs.def:centered-moments: For r in {2,3,4}, m_r(nu) = integral_[0,1] (u - pbar)^r d nu(u).

thm:finite-attainability.proof_tex: Proof. Fix a finite atomic admissible law nu = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell} in N(pbar). By def:admissible-law-class, the weights are nonnegative, sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell = 1, each u_ell lies in [0,1], and sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell u_ell = pbar. Assume M w_ell is an integer for every ell and m u_ell is an integer for every ell. Put n_ell = M w_ell. Then n_ell is a nonnegative integer and sum_{ell=1}^K n_ell = M sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell = M. Form a saturation vector pi in [0,1]^M by listing the value u_ell exactly n_ell times, for ell = 1,...,K. Since m u_ell is an integer and 0 <= u_ell <= 1, every coordinate pi_j lies in the grid {0,1/m,2/m,...,1}. Its average saturation is M^{-1} sum_{j=1}^M pi_j = M^{-1} sum_{ell=1}^K n_ell u_ell = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell u_ell = pbar. Hence pi belongs to Pi_{M,m}(pbar) by def:implementable-vector-class. By def:empirical-law, L_M(pi) = M^{-1} sum_{j=1}^M delta_{pi_j} = sum_{ell=1}^K (n_ell/M) delta_{u_ell} = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell} = nu. This proves exact finite attainability. The same divisibility conditions imply N pbar is an integer: using N = M m from the equal-cluster-size setup, N pbar = M m sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell u_ell = sum_{ell=1}^K (M w_ell)(m u_ell), an integer sum of integer products. For the Bernoulli vertex nu_B = (1-pbar) delta_0 + pbar delta_1, the support-grid conditions m*0 in Z and m*1 in Z are automatic. The mass-count conditions are M(1-pbar) in Z and M pbar in Z; because M is an integer, these are equivalent to M pbar in Z. Conversely, if an empirical law equals nu_B, exactly M pbar of the M coordinates must be equal to 1, so M pbar must be an integer. Thus exact attainability of the Bernoulli vertex reduces precisely to M pbar in Z. For the within-cluster stratified law delta_pbar, the mass condition M*1 in Z is automatic, and the support-grid condition is exactly m pbar in Z. Conversely, if L_M(pi) = delta_pbar, then every coordinate pi_j must equal pbar; since pi in Pi_{M,m}(pbar) has grid-valued coordinates, m pbar must be an integer. Thus exact attainability of delta_pbar reduces precisely to m pbar in Z.

snapshot.stmt: Let nu = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell} be in N(pbar). If M w_ell is an integer for every ell and m u_ell is an integer for every ell, then there exists pi in Pi_{M,m}(pbar) such that L_M(pi) = nu. In particular, these divisibility conditions already imply N pbar in Z. For the Bernoulli vertex (1 - pbar) delta_0 + pbar delta_1, exact attainability reduces to M pbar in Z. For the within-cluster stratified law delta_pbar, exact attainability reduces to m pbar in Z.

defs.def:admissible-law-class: For pbar in [0,1], N(pbar) = {nu in P([0,1]) : integral_[0,1] u d nu(u) = pbar}.
defs.def:implementable-vector-class: For pbar in [0,1], Pi_{M,m}(pbar) = {pi in {0, 1/m, 2/m, ..., 1}^M : M^(-1) sum_{j=1}^M pi_j = pbar}.

assumptions.ass:equal-cluster-size: The finite population is partitioned into M clusters C_1, ..., C_M of common size m.

thm:rounding-loss.proof_tex: Let nu_star in N(pbar) have support cardinality K <= 3, and write nu_star = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell} with w_ell > 0 and u_ell in [0,1]. Let N = M m and assume N pbar is an integer. Apply lem:finite-atomic-grid-split-rounding with nu = nu_star. It gives a vector pi_rd in Pi_{M,m}(pbar), produced by the grid-split rounding construction, and a coupling gamma_rd of nu_star with L_M(pi_rd) whose average displacement is at most 6/M + 2/m. Apply lem:quartic-variance-coupling-lipschitz to this coupling. With L_V = 2 |V_1| + 3 |V_3| + 8 |V_4|, we obtain |V(L_M(pi_rd)) - V(nu_star)| <= L_V (6/M + 2/m). Hence V(L_M(pi_rd)) <= V(nu_star) + 6 L_V (M^{-1} + m^{-1}). Taking the rounding constant C_rd(nu_star) to be any admissible constant at least 6 L_V, for instance C_rd(nu_star)=6(2 |V_1| + 3 |V_3| + 8 |V_4|), gives the stated inequality. The proof uses only the support-size premise on nu_star; prop:support-at-most-three supplies that premise when nu_star is chosen as a continuous optimizer.

snapshot.stmt: For every nu_star in N(pbar) with card(supp(nu_star)) <= 3 and N pbar in Z, the grid-split rounding handle produces pi_rd in Pi_{M,m}(pbar) such that V(L_M(pi_rd)) <= V(nu_star) + C_rd(nu_star) (M^(-1) + m^(-1)).

defs.def:grid-split-rounding: Given a K-atomic law nu = sum_{ell=1}^K w_ell delta_{u_ell}, first round the masses w_ell to a vector of cluster counts summing to M, then replace each support point u_ell by one or two adjacent points in {0,1/m,...,1} with mixing weights chosen to preserve the contribution of that atom to the mean constraint up to the final balancing adjustment that enforces M^(-1) sum_j pi_j = pbar.
defs.def:implementable-vector-class: For pbar in [0,1], Pi_{M,m}(pbar) = {pi in {0, 1/m, 2/m, ..., 1}^M : M^(-1) sum_{j=1}^M pi_j = pbar}.

prop:sutva-corner.proof_tex: Proof. If V_3=V_4=0, def:variance-functional gives V(nu)=V_0+V_1 m_2(nu) for every nu in N(pbar). By lem:centered-second-moment-extrema, 0<=m_2(nu)<=pbar(1-pbar). The lower endpoint is attained by delta_pbar and, when the budget is interior, only by delta_pbar. The upper endpoint is attained by the Bernoulli vertex (1-pbar)delta_0+pbar delta_1 and, for 0<pbar<1, only by that vertex. Therefore V_1>0 makes the lower endpoint delta_pbar optimal, V_1<0 makes the upper endpoint Bernoulli vertex optimal, and V_1=0 makes V identically equal to V_0 on N(pbar), so all admissible laws tie and in particular the extremal-m_2 laws tie. The exact finite-realization clauses are exactly the two specializations of thm:finite-attainability: the Bernoulli vertex requires and is required by M pbar in Z, whereas the within-cluster stratified law delta_pbar requires and is required by m pbar in Z.

snapshot.stmt: If V_3 = 0 and V_4 = 0, then V(nu) = V_0 + V_1 m_2(nu) for every nu in N(pbar). Hence V_1 > 0 implies delta_pbar minimizes V over nu in N(pbar), V_1 < 0 implies (1 - pbar) delta_0 + pbar delta_1 minimizes V over nu in N(pbar), and V_1 = 0 makes every nu in N(pbar) tie in V, hence all extremal-m_2 laws tie accordingly. The exact finite realization of the Bernoulli vertex uses M pbar in Z, whereas the exact finite realization of delta_pbar uses m pbar in Z.

defs.def:admissible-law-class: For pbar in [0,1], N(pbar) = {nu in P([0,1]) : integral_[0,1] u d nu(u) = pbar}.
defs.def:centered-moments: For r in {2,3,4}, m_r(nu) = integral_[0,1] (u - pbar)^r d nu(u).

prop:design-optimal-saturation.proof_tex:
Proof. Under ass:equal-cluster-size, ass:isolated-partial-interference, ass:anonymous-share-specialization, ass:homogeneous-linear-share-working-model, and ass:cai-leading-expansion-domain, lem:variance-moment-reduction identifies the standard o(N^{-1})-free leading randomized-saturation variance criterion associated with any admissible law nu as V(nu). Therefore a law is leading-design-variance-optimal over N(pbar) if and only if it minimizes V over N(pbar). The minimizers of V are characterized exactly by thm:global-certificate, so the same certificate characterizes leading design-variance optimality in the model regime of the bridge lemma.

If 0<pbar<1, prop:positive-penalty-threshold applies to the pure moment program and gives

delta_pbar in argmin_{nu in N(pbar)} V(nu) if and only if min_{d in D_pbar} (V_1+V_3 d+V_4 d^2) >= 0.

Combining this equivalence with the preceding leading-criterion identification gives the stated if and only if for leading-design-variance optimality of the stratified law delta_pbar. If pbar=0 or pbar=1, lem:boundary-admissible-law-singleton gives N(pbar)={delta_pbar}, and delta_pbar is automatically leading-design-variance-optimal. Finally, when V_3=V_4=0, prop:sutva-corner gives the SUTVA degeneration of the same V-minimization problem, including the stratified, Bernoulli-vertex, tie, and finite-compatibility clauses. Since the bridge lemma deliberately identifies only the leading o(N^{-1})-free criterion, the exact non-leading finite-population variance remains a separate scope item.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:equal-cluster-size, ass:isolated-partial-interference, ass:anonymous-share-specialization, ass:homogeneous-linear-share-working-model, and ass:cai-leading-expansion-domain, by lem:variance-moment-reduction the standard leading design-variance-optimal saturation law over N(pbar) is exactly the V-minimizer. Hence leading design-variance optimality is characterized by thm:global-certificate. If 0 < pbar < 1, then the stratified design delta_pbar is leading-design-variance-optimal if and only if min_{d in D_pbar} (V_1 + V_3 d + V_4 d^2) >= 0 by prop:positive-penalty-threshold; if pbar in {0,1}, then N(pbar) = {delta_pbar}, so delta_pbar is automatically leading-design-variance-optimal. The SUTVA degeneration is given by prop:sutva-corner. The exact non-leading finite-population variance remains a separate scope item.

lem:explicit-profile-value.proof_tex: Proof. Work in centered coordinates X=u-p with p=pbar, q=1-p, C=pq, A=q-p, and D=[-p,q], using lem:centered-pushforward-program. If s=0, every centered law in the slice has X=0 almost surely, so F(0)=0. If s=C, E[(X+p)(q-X)]=C-s=0 and the nonnegative factor (X+p)(q-X) forces support on {-p,q}; both displayed intervals collapse to the common value A and the two displayed formulas coincide with the endpoint law value. Now let 0<s<C. For any law in the slice, put m3=E X^3 and m4=E X^4. The lemma lem:fixed-second-moment-envelope gives t=m3/s in I_2(s), r=(m3-A s)/(s-C) in I_3(s), the lower boundary m4>=s^2+s t^2, attained by the two-root law, and the upper boundary m4<=(1-3C)s+(s-C)(r^2+A r), attained by the endpoint-interior law. If V_4>=0, then for the law under consideration V_1 s+V_3 m3+V_4 m4 is at least Phi_2(s,t), and the two-root law attains Phi_2(s,t) for every t in I_2(s). Hence the slice infimum is min_{t in I_2(s)} Phi_2(s,t). In this case every endpoint-interior boundary point has the same m3 as some two-root law and weakly larger m4, so its value is no smaller; thus the minimum over the union of the two displayed branches is the same number. If V_4<0, the same argument with the upper envelope gives the slice infimum as min_{r in I_3(s)} Phi_3(s,r), and every two-root boundary point is dominated by the endpoint-interior law with the same m3 and weakly larger m4; again this equals the minimum over the union of the two branches. Substituting m3=s t and m4=s^2+s t^2 gives Phi_2, and substituting m3=A s+(s-C)r and the upper-envelope value for m4 gives Phi_3. Therefore F(s)=min{min_{t in I_2(s)} Phi_2(s,t), min_{r in I_3(s)} Phi_3(s,r)}. Finally, def:outer-certificate gives H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4 s^2. Each displayed minimization is a quadratic polynomial over a closed interval with endpoints rational in s and p, so the value is obtained by checking endpoints and the algebraic stationary point when it lies in the interval. Hence F and H are explicit piecewise-algebraic functions of the stated parameters.

snapshot.stmt: For 0 < pbar < 1, write p=pbar, q=1-p, C=pq, and A=q-p. For s in [0,C], let I_2(s)=[s/p-p, q-s/q] and I_3(s)=[-s/q, s/p]. Define Phi_2(s,t)=V_1 s+V_4 s^2+s(V_3 t+V_4 t^2) and Phi_3(s,r)=V_1 s+V_3(A s+(s-C)r)+V_4((1-3C)s+(s-C)(r^2+A r)). Then the fixed-second-moment profile satisfies F(0)=0 and, for all s in [0,C], F(s)=min{min_{t in I_2(s)} Phi_2(s,t), min_{r in I_3(s)} Phi_3(s,r)}; consequently H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4s^2. The first minimum is attained by the two-point centered law whose support points are the roots of x^2-tx-s=0. The second is attained by the endpoint-interior law on {-p,r,q} with weights (s+rq)/(p+r), (C-s)/((p+r)(q-r)), and (s-pr)/(q-r), with the endpoint limiting interpretation. Since the two displayed minimizations are quadratic over closed intervals, F and H are explicit piecewise-algebraic functions of (s,pbar,V_1,V_3,V_4).

defs.def:centered-moments: For r in {2,3,4}, m_r(nu) = integral_[0,1] (u - pbar)^r d nu(u).
defs.def:profile-value: F(s) = inf_{nu in N_s(pbar,s)} integral_[0,1] [V_1 (u - pbar)^2 + V_3 (u - pbar)^3 + V_4 (u - pbar)^4] d nu(u).
defs.def:outer-certificate: For the necessary-and-sufficient global certificate, the outer certificate is the profiled value H(s) = V_0 + F(s) - V_4 s^2. The affine expression G^{lin}_{a,c}(s)=V_0+a+c s-V_4s^2 is only a sufficient lower-certificate obtained from one slice dual line; it is not necessary for global optimality.

thm:constructive-optimal-design-algorith.proof_tex: Proof. Let theta=(pbar,V_1,V_3,V_4). If pbar=0 and nu in N(pbar), then integral u dnu(u)=0 while u>=0, so nu is delta_0. If pbar=1, then integral (1-u) dnu(u)=0 while 1-u>=0, so nu is delta_1. Thus the boundary algorithm that outputs delta_pbar is terminating, feasible, and globally optimal, and the certificate is the direct singleton certificate. Assume now that 0<pbar<1. Put p=pbar, q=1-p, C=pq, A=q-p, and D=[-p,q]. By lem:centered-pushforward-program, minimizing V over N(pbar) is equivalent to minimizing over probability laws mu on D with integral d dmu(d)=0, after the change of variables d=u-p. By prop:support-at-most-three, at least one global minimizer has support size at most three. By lem:profile-value-reduction, on a fixed second-moment slice s the objective is V_0+integral f(d)dmu(d)-V_4s^2, where f(d)=V_1d^2+V_3d^3+V_4d^4. By lem:explicit-profile-value, the slice infimum is explicit: F(0)=0 and, for 0<=s<=C, F(s) is the smaller of the two displayed quadratic interval minima Phi_2 over I_2(s) and Phi_3 over I_3(s), and H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4s^2. The Phi_2 branch is attained by the two-root law x^2-tx-s=0; when optimized over all two-point laws it is equivalently represented by the singleton S1 and the two endpoint cubic branches S2, by lem:two-point-endpoint-reduction. The Phi_3 branch is attained by the endpoint-interior law on {-p,r,q} with the weights stated in lem:explicit-profile-value; this is the three-point family S3, with its boundary subfamilies obtained by allowing active inequalities in 0<=s<=C and -s/q<=r<=s/p. Therefore the global value of the law program is the minimum over the finite union of the branch programs S1, S2, and S3 described in the statement. Each branch has a compact semialgebraic feasible set after multiplying inequalities by the positive constants p and q, and each branch objective is polynomial in the branch variables and the exact input parameters. The finitely many endpoint, active-boundary, and stationary equations listed in the statement are precisely the KKT equations for these one-dimensional or two-dimensional polynomial branch objectives on their compact semialgebraic domains. By lem:semialgebraic-optimization-algorithms, finite polynomial optimization over these compact semialgebraic sets admits a terminating exact procedure using Tarski-Seidenberg elimination, CAD, Thom encodings, root isolation, and Sturm sign tests; the procedure returns feasible algebraic candidates, discards infeasible roots by exact sign evaluation, orders their objective values exactly, and selects a minimum. Construct nu_alg from the selected branch: S1 gives delta_pbar; S2 uses the endpoint two-point formulas; S3 uses the endpoint-interior weights from lem:explicit-profile-value and then pushes forward by d -> p+d. The constructed law lies in N(pbar), and its exact algebraic V-value equals the selected branch value. Since the branch union contains a support-at-most-three global minimizer and the branch values equal the profile values supplied by lem:explicit-profile-value, no admissible law has V-value below V(nu_alg). Hence nu_alg minimizes V over N(pbar). Finally, the stated certificate is finite and exact. Direct boundary and degenerate-branch cases are certified by singleton feasibility or exact candidate comparison. On an interior contact pattern, the contact and tangency equations determine multipliers a,b,c for Q_{a,b,c}; nonnegativity of the quartic residual on D is decidable by Sturm/root-isolation because Q is a univariate quartic, and the outer inequality H(s)>=V(nu_alg) on [0,C] is decidable by the same semialgebraic machinery using the explicit piecewise-algebraic formula for H from lem:explicit-profile-value. When these checks hold, thm:global-certificate gives an independent global-optimality certificate. Thus the algorithm is complete, exact, terminating, returns a globally optimal saturation law, and certifies it; the separate task of compressing the exact CAD/Thom output into a short printed atlas is not part of this theorem.

snapshot.stmt: There is a complete, exact, terminating constructive optimizer for the quartic saturation program. The input is theta=(pbar,V_1,V_3,V_4) with 0<=pbar<=1, and the output is a law nu_alg in N(pbar) minimizing V. If pbar is 0 or 1, output delta_pbar; this boundary case is certified directly because N(pbar) is then the singleton {delta_pbar}. If 0<pbar<1, write p=pbar, q=1-p, C=pq, and A=q-p, then enumerate the finite support/contact subsystems: S1, the singleton centered support {0}; S2, the two-point centered supports {-b,a}, equivalently the endpoint cubic branches g_L(t)=p(p+t)(V_1+V_3t+V_4t^2) on [-p,A] and g_R(t)=q(q-t)(V_1+V_3t+V_4t^2) on [A,q] together with their endpoint and stationary equations; and S3, the three-point endpoint-interior centered supports {-p,r,q}, equivalently the KKT systems for the explicit profile branch Phi_3 over 0<=s<=C and -s/q<=r<=s/p, including every active boundary subset. Solve these finitely many polynomial systems by exact real-algebraic elimination, cylindrical algebraic decomposition, Thom encodings, and root isolation; discard infeasible roots; construct the associated laws and exact algebraic V-values; and choose a minimum. The chosen law is certified by exact candidate comparison and, on interior contact patterns, by constructing the quartic residual Q, checking Q>=0 on D_pbar by Sturm/root isolation, and checking the outer inequality H(s)>=V(nu_alg) on [0,C] using the explicit closed-form H(s)=V_0+F(s)-V_4s^2. Exact algebraic CAD/Thom computation is the constructive method; the separate atlas question is only whether this exact output can be compressed into compact human-readable regions.

defs.def:admissible-law-class: For pbar in [0,1], N(pbar) = {nu in P([0,1]) : integral_[0,1] u d nu(u) = pbar}.

prop:explicit-two-point-cell.proof_tex: Proof. Assume 0<pbar<1 and write p=pbar, q=1-p, A=q-p, D=[-p,q], and q_theta(t)=V_1+V_3t+V_4t^2. In centered coordinates, the only one-point mean-zero law is delta_0, hence its value above V_0 is 0. A nondegenerate two-point mean-zero law has support {a,-b} with 0<a<=q and 0<b<=p, mass b/(a+b) at a, and mass a/(a+b) at -b. By lem:two-point-centered-objective, with t=a-b its value above V_0 is ab q_theta(t). By lem:two-point-endpoint-reduction, the minimum over all singleton and two-point centered laws is therefore the minimum of the singleton value 0, the left endpoint branch obtained by setting b=p, and the right endpoint branch obtained by setting a=q. The left endpoint branch has a=p+t and t in [-p,A], so its value is g_L(t)=p(p+t)q_theta(t). The right endpoint branch has b=q-t and t in [A,q], so its value is g_R(t)=q(q-t)q_theta(t). These two compact intervals include all endpoint and degenerate cases, with t=A representing the common two-endpoint law. Since g_L and g_R are cubic polynomials on compact intervals, their minima occur at endpoints or at roots of their derivatives. Direct differentiation gives g_L'(t)=p[V_1+pV_3+2(V_3+pV_4)t+3V_4t^2] and g_R'(t)=q[-V_1+qV_3+2(qV_4-V_3)t-3V_4t^2]. Because p and q are positive, the stationary equations are exactly the two equations displayed in the statement. Thus the complete candidate set is t=-p,A,q together with the roots of the left derivative equation that lie in [-p,A] and the roots of the right derivative equation that lie in [A,q]. Choosing the candidate with the smallest exact value gives the two-point-class optimum. If the selected value is 0, the law delta_pbar attains it. If the selected candidate lies on the left branch, its centered support is {-p,p+t}; the mean-zero weights are (p+t)/(2p+t) at -p and p/(2p+t) at p+t. If the selected candidate lies on the right branch, its centered support is {t-q,q}; the mean-zero weights are q/(2q-t) at t-q and (q-t)/(2q-t) at q. These formulas are obtained by solving the single equation that the weighted centered mean is zero, and the denominators are positive on the stated intervals except for degenerate endpoints, where the zero-weight atom is removed. The comparison between this closed-form singleton/two-point optimum and possible three-point endpoint-interior optima is not part of the two-point optimization itself; it is exactly the algebraic comparison carried out by thm:constructive-optimal-design-algorithm.

snapshot.stmt: The exact optimizer over the singleton/two-point saturation class is closed form. Let p=pbar, q=1-p, A=q-p, and q_theta(t)=V_1+V_3t+V_4t^2. The value above V_0 is the minimum of 0, the left endpoint cubic branch g_L(t)=p(p+t)q_theta(t) on t in [-p,A], and the right endpoint cubic branch g_R(t)=q(q-t)q_theta(t) on t in [A,q]. The complete candidate set is t=-p,A,q together with the roots in [-p,A] of V_1+pV_3+2(V_3+pV_4)t+3V_4t^2=0 and the roots in [A,q] of -V_1+qV_3+2(qV_4-V_3)t-3V_4t^2=0. Choosing the candidate with smallest value gives the two-point-class optimum: value 0 gives delta_pbar; a left-branch minimizer gives centered support {-p,p+t} with weights (p+t)/(2p+t) at -p and p/(2p+t) at p+t; and a right-branch minimizer gives centered support {t-q,q} with weights q/(2q-t) at t-q and (q-t)/(2q-t) at q. The two-versus-three-point comparison is the exact algebraic comparison performed in thm:constructive-optimal-design-algorithm and recorded in oeq:full-branch-optimizer-map; it is not a missing piece of this complete two-point result.

oeq:full-branch-optimizer-map.proof_tex: For every theta=(pbar,V_1,V_3,V_4) with 0<=pbar<=1, thm:constructive-optimal-design-algorithm returns a globally optimal saturation law supported on at most three points and certifies it. The profile H(s) is explicit by lem:explicit-profile-value, and the singleton/two-point cells are closed form by prop:explicit-two-point-cell.

snapshot.stmt: Certified delivered content plus the remaining atlas-compression question. Over Theta = {(pbar, V_1, V_3, V_4) : 0 <= pbar <= 1}, thm:constructive-optimal-design-algorithm gives a terminating exact algebraic procedure that outputs a globally optimal saturation law and certifies it; lem:explicit-profile-value gives a closed-form profiled H(s); and prop:explicit-two-point-cell gives the complete closed-form optimizer over the singleton/two-point class, with the exact algebraic comparison used by the constructive algorithm to decide global activation. The remaining open question is to simplify the full constructive/CAD output into a human-readable symbolic region-by-region atlas with compact interpretable inequalities and closed-form formulas for every three-point cell.

defs.def:branch-map-contact-handle: Let Theta={theta=(pbar,V_1,V_3,V_4):0<=pbar<=1}. For k in {1,2,3}, define E_k(theta) as the set of tuples (w_1,...,w_k,d_1,...,d_k) satisfying w_i>=0, sum_i w_i=1, -pbar<=d_1<=...<=d_k<=1-pbar, and sum_i w_i d_i=0. Define the branch objective R_k^0(theta,w,d)=V_1 sum_i w_i d_i^2+V_3 sum_i w_i d_i^3+V_4{sum_i w_i d_i^4-(sum_i w_i d_i^2)^2}; equivalently one may add the common constant V_0, which does not affect argmin comparisons. A tuple (theta,w,d,k) is globally active when (w,d) in E_k(theta) and, for every l in {1,2,3} and every (w',d') in E_l(theta), R_k^0(theta,w,d)<=R_l^0(theta,w',d'). Apply Tarski-Seidenberg quantifier elimination, followed by cylindrical algebraic decomposition with Thom encodings, to the finite family of active-branch formulas. This yields a finite semialgebraic partition Theta=union_r C_r and, on each cell C_r, a selected support size k_r and Thom-encoded algebraic branch functions w_{ri}(theta),d_{ri}(theta). The branch optimizer map is B(theta)=sum_{i=1}^{k_r} w_{ri}(theta) delta_{pbar+d_{ri}(theta)} for theta in C_r, with zero-weight atoms removed and duplicate support points merged.
